Star on GitHub Get started

Compare

Recurso vs Kill Bill

Both are permissively licensed open-source billing platforms — no AGPL debate here. The real trade-off is maturity versus operational weight: Kill Bill has a decade-plus of production hardening and a deep plugin ecosystem; Recurso is a young, lightweight Go + PostgreSQL engine that's far quicker to stand up.

Start self-hosting Read the quickstart

MIT licensed · Go + PostgreSQL + TigerBeetle · Stripe & Razorpay

At a glance

Young and lightweight vs mature and heavyweight

Kill Bill is Apache 2.0, Java, and battle-tested. Recurso is MIT, Go, and easy to operate — but far younger.

Dimension Recurso Kill Bill
License MIT Apache 2.0Also permissive — no copyleft concern
Maturity New project, small community 10+ years in production, proven at scale — a real advantage
Stack Go + PostgreSQL, single binary Java (JVM), typically with a servlet container + database
Operational weight Light — one binary plus PostgreSQL Heavyweight to deploy, tune, and operate
Extensibility Configuration + API; MIT source you can fork freely Mature plugin architecture with a broad plugin catalog — a genuine strength
Accounting ledger Built-in double-entry ledger; optional TigerBeetle mirror Invoice + payment accounting; account balance model
India compliance GST e-invoicing (IRP), RBI e-mandates with 24-hour pre-debit notices, DPDP consent — built in Not built in; would need custom work or plugins
Dunning Algorithmic retries (epsilon-greedy, Thompson, UCB1) with recovered-revenue attribution Configurable payment retry (overdue) logic
API & SDKs OpenAPI 3.1 (113 paths), Node + Python SDKs Mature REST API with client libraries in several languages
Software cost, self-hosted $0Gateway fees still apply $0Gateway fees still apply

Competitor column reflects Kill Bill's publicly stated positioning as of mid-2026. Verify specifics against their current documentation.

Architecture

Maturity vs operational simplicity

This is the crux. Kill Bill's decade of hardening is real; so is the weight that comes with it.

Kill Bill: mature Java + plugins

Kill Bill has been in production for over ten years and has earned trust at serious scale. Its plugin architecture is a legitimate strength: payment gateways, tax, analytics, and custom logic all slot in through well-defined extension points, and there's a real catalog to draw from.

The cost is operational weight. It's a JVM application that is heavier to deploy, tune, and keep running than a single static binary — you're taking on more moving parts and more expertise to run it well. For many teams that's a perfectly reasonable trade for the maturity.

Recurso: light Go + PostgreSQL

Recurso is one Go binary against PostgreSQL, with an optional TigerBeetle ledger mirror (PostgreSQL stays authoritative). There's no JVM to tune and no servlet container to manage — you can be running in minutes with a small ops footprint.

On our benchmark hardware: ~7.8k authenticated reads/s and ~34.6k invoices/min with full ledger posting. The scripts ship in the repo — reproduce the numbers on your own hardware.

Where Recurso is honestly behind

Kill Bill's ten-plus years of production hardening, its plugin ecosystem, and its proven scale are things a young project simply hasn't earned yet. Recurso has a small community and no hosted cloud, EU VAT is a static rate table, US sales tax runs through TaxJar behind a key, and nexus isn't modeled. If maturity and a deep plugin catalog are your priorities, Kill Bill has the stronger claim.

The honest verdict

Which one should you run?

Choose Kill Bill if…

  • A decade-plus of production maturity and proven scale is your top priority
  • You want a broad, established plugin ecosystem to extend billing
  • Your team is JVM-native and comfortable operating Java services at scale
  • You don't need India-native compliance out of the box

Choose Recurso if…

  • You want a light ops footprint: one Go binary plus PostgreSQL, running in minutes
  • You bill Indian customers and want GST, RBI e-mandates, and DPDP consent built in
  • You want algorithmic dunning with recovered-revenue attribution built in, not bolted on
  • You prefer a Go codebase and a modern OpenAPI 3.1 spec with Node + Python SDKs
  • You want a permissive license — both qualify, but MIT is as unencumbered as it gets
vs Lago vs Flexprice vs Chargebee vs Stripe Billing

Try Recurso in one command

Free forever on your own infrastructure. Clone the repo and you're minutes away from a seeded dashboard.

Start self-hosting Read the quickstart

git clone https://github.com/swapnull-in/recur-so.git && make demo

recurso

The open-source billing engine for SaaS. Built with Go, PostgreSQL, and TigerBeetle. MIT licensed.
